steps.title: Identify the grammatical structure
steps.content: The blank contains the subject of the finite verb compares. It's composed of a noun (“playwright”) followed by a name (“Wakako Yamauchi”) in apposition.

steps.title: Apply the appositive punctuation rule
steps.content:
When an appositive is restrictive — essential to identify the noun — it takes no commas. When it's non-restrictive — providing additional information — it requires commas on both sides. Here, “Wakako Yamauchi” is essential to identify the specific playwright, making it restrictive and requiring no commas. Additionally, no comma can separate the subject from its verb.

Options A and C violate the subject-verb rule by placing a comma before compares, while Option B incorrectly treats the name as non-restrictive by using a comma after “playwright.”

Option D correctly applies the restrictive appositive rule: no commas around the name and no comma before the verb. That matches choice D — D is correct.
